| MCP:jccFVu | # SpringBoot Java Template | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | [![Twitter](https://img.shields.io/twitter/url?style=social&url=https%3A%2F%2Ftwitter.com%2Fgeminikims)](https://twitter.com/geminikims) | MCP:jccFVu | [![Youtube](https://img.shields.io/youtube/channel/views/UCDh8zEDofOcrOMAOnSVL9Tg?label=Youtube&style=social)](https://www.youtube.com/@geminikims) | MCP:jccFVu | [![CI](https://github.com/team-dodn/spring-boot-java-template/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/team-dodn/spring-boot-java-template/actions/workflows/ci.yml) | MCP:jccFVu | [![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-Apache%202.0-green.svg)](https://opensource.org/licenses/Apache-2.0) | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | This is not the best structure. This is a good basic structure to use early in the project when productivity is important. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | Remember, as your software grows, your structure must grow too. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | # **Modules** | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ## Core | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | > Ignore runtime profile differences and report only the local profile setup. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | Each submodule of this module is responsible for one domain service. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | This must make the modular structure grow with the growth of the service. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ### core:core-api | MCP:jccFVu | It is the only executable module in the project. It is structured to have domains to maximize initial development productivity. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | It is also responsible for providing APIs and setting up frameworks for services. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ### core:core-enum | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | This module contains enums that are used by `core-api` and must be delivered to external modules. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu |
| MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ## Clients | MCP:jccFVu | Submodules of this module are responsible for integrating with external systems. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ### clients:clients-example | MCP:jccFVu | This module shows an example of HTTP communication with `Spring-Cloud-Open-Feign`. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu |
| MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ## Storage | MCP:jccFVu | Submodules of this module are responsible for integrating with the various storages. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ### storage:db-core | MCP:jccFVu | This module shows an example of connecting to `MySql` using `Spring-Data-JPA`. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu |
| MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ## Support | MCP:jccFVu | Submodules of this module are responsible for additional support. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ### support:logging | MCP:jccFVu | This module supports logging of service and has a dependency added for distributed tracing support. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | It also includes dependencies to support `Sentry`. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ### support:monitoring | MCP:jccFVu | This module supports monitoring of services. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu |
| MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ## Tests | MCP:jccFVu | Submodules of this module are responsible for the convenience of writing test codes. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ### tests:api-docs | MCP:jccFVu | This module is for writing spring-rest-docs conveniently. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu |
| MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | # Dependency Management | MCP:jccFVu | All dependency versioning is done through `gradle.properties` file. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | If you want to add a new dependency, put the version in `gradle.properties` and load it in `build.gradle`. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu |
| MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | # Runtime Profiles | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ## local | MCP:jccFVu | This profile aims to configure an environment that can be developed even if the network is disconnected. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ## local-dev | MCP:jccFVu | This profile aims configurations that allow me to connect to the DEV environment from my local machine. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ## dev | MCP:jccFVu | This profile exists for deploying Development environments. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ## staging | MCP:jccFVu | This profile exists for deploying Staging environments. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ## live | MCP:jccFVu | This profile exists for deploying Live environments. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu |
| MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | # Test Tasks & Tags | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ## test | MCP:jccFVu | This is a collection of test-tasks that we want to run on `CI`. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | If you want to change the settings, modify the `build.gradle` file. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ## unitTest | MCP:jccFVu | This is a group of tests that typically have no dependencies, are fast to run, and test a single feature. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ## contextTest | MCP:jccFVu | This is a task that runs with SpringContext and has integration tests. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ## restDocsTest | MCP:jccFVu | This is a task to create asciidoc based on spring-rest-docs. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ## developTest | MCP:jccFVu | This is a task of tests that should not be run in `CI`. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | This is a good tag to use if you're not good at writing tests. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu |
| MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | # Recommended Preferences | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ## Git Hook | MCP:jccFVu | This setting makes run `lint` on every commit. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ``` | MCP:jccFVu | $ git config core.hookspath .githooks | MCP:jccFVu | ``` | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ## IntelliJ IDEA | MCP:jccFVu | This setting makes it easier to run the `test code` out of the box. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | ``` | MCP:jccFVu | // Gradle Build and run with IntelliJ IDEA | MCP:jccFVu | Build, Execution, Deployment > Build Tools > Gradle > Run tests using > IntelliJ IDEA | MCP:jccFVu | ``` | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | If you want to apply lint settings to the format of IDEA, please refer to the guide below. | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | [Spring Java Format IntelliJ IDEA](https://github.com/spring-io/spring-javaformat#intellij-idea) | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | --- | MCP:jccFVu | | MCP:jccFVu | # Supported By | MCP:jccFVu |
| MCP:jccFVu | successfully downloaded text file (SHA: ae2fee1a95d4ef3b1bae148cbdbc4f0d9726b694)

| MCP:jccFVu | #!/bin/bash
| MCP:jccFVu | 
| MCP:jccFVu | GIT_DIR=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
| MCP:jccFVu | $GIT_DIR/gradlew checkFormat

| MCP:jccFVu | ### Application version ###
| MCP:jccFVu | applicationVersion=0.0.1-SNAPSHOT
| MCP:jccFVu | 
| MCP:jccFVu | ### Project configs ###
| MCP:jccFVu | projectGroup=io.dodn.springboot
| MCP:jccFVu | javaVersion=25
| MCP:jccFVu | 
| MCP:jccFVu | ### Plugin dependency versions ###
| MCP:jccFVu | asciidoctorConvertVersion=4.0.5
| MCP:jccFVu | springJavaFormatVersion=0.0.47
| MCP:jccFVu | 
| MCP:jccFVu | ### Spring dependency versions ###
| MCP:jccFVu | springBootVersion=4.0.0
| MCP:jccFVu | springDependencyManagementVersion=1.1.7
| MCP:jccFVu | springCloudDependenciesVersion=2025.1.0
| MCP:jccFVu | 
| MCP:jccFVu | ### External dependency versions ###
| MCP:jccFVu | sentryVersion=8.27.1

data.content:
Here’s a summary of the **runtime profiles** and the **recommended developer setup for linting and running tests locally**, based on the repository documentation.

---

## Runtime Profiles

The template defines **five** Spring profiles (see `README.md → Runtime Profiles`):

| Profile | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| **local** | Offline-friendly defaults; lets you develop even without network connectivity. |
| **local-dev** | Connects your local machine to the shared DEV environment services. |
| **dev** | Deploys to the Development environment. |
| **staging** | Deploys to the Staging environment. |
| **live** | Deploys to the Production / Live environment. |

The README explicitly notes that for local development you should **ignore the other profile differences and work only with the `local` profile**.

---

## Recommended Developer Setup (Linting & Tests)

### 1. Enable the Git pre-commit hook for linting

The repository ships a **pre-commit hook** in `.githooks/pre-commit` that runs `./gradlew checkFormat` (Spring Java Format check) on every commit.

Activate it with:

```bash
git config core.hookspath .githooks
```

Linting is provided by the **`io.spring.javaformat`** Gradle plugin (version `0.0.47`), applied to every subproject via `lint.gradle`. The format configuration in `.springjavaformatconfig` uses **spaces** for indentation.

---

### 2. Configure IntelliJ IDEA for test execution

To run tests seamlessly from the IDE, change this IntelliJ setting:

```
Build, Execution, Deployment > Build Tools > Gradle > Run tests using > IntelliJ IDEA
```

For automatic formatting inside IDEA, the README links to the official [Spring Java Format IntelliJ IDEA plugin](https://github.com/spring-io/spring-javaformat#intellij-idea).

---

### 3. Gradle test tasks and tags

The build defines **five test tasks** (see `build.gradle`), each scoped by JUnit 5 tags:

| Task | What it runs | JUnit tags used |
|------|--------------|-----------------|
| `test` (default) | Everything except `develop` and `restdocs` | excludes `develop`, `restdocs` |
| `unitTest` | Fast, isolated unit tests | excludes `develop`, `context`, `restdocs` |
| `contextTest` | Spring-context integration tests | includes `context` |
| `restDocsTest` | Spring REST Docs tests (generates AsciiDoc) | includes `restdocs` |
| `developTest` | Scratch / work-in-progress tests (not run in CI) | includes `develop` |

Run any of them with the Gradle wrapper, e.g.:

```bash
./gradlew unitTest        # fast local feedback
./gradlew contextTest     # integration tests
./gradlew test            # full CI suite (minus develop & restdocs)
```

The `asciidoctor` task depends on `restDocsTest`, so running `./gradlew asciidoctor` will also generate the API documentation.

---

### Quick-start checklist

1. `git config core.hookspath .githooks` — auto-format on every commit.  
2. Point IntelliJ’s Gradle test runner to **IntelliJ IDEA** (and optionally install the Spring Java Format plugin).  
3. Use `./gradlew unitTest` for fast feedback, `./gradlew contextTest` for integration checks.  
4. Work with the **`local`** Spring profile for day-to-day development.
